---
title: "Zephyr metagenomic biosurveillance: taxonomy, coverage, and read clustering"
author: "Amadeu Rubiés"
date: "`r format(Sys.Date())`"
output:
  html_document:
    toc: true
    toc_depth: 2
    pandoc_args: ["--resource-path", ".."]
  word_document:
    toc: false
    pandoc_args: ["--resource-path", ".."]
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = FALSE, message = FALSE, warning = FALSE)
suppressPackageStartupMessages({ library(data.table); library(knitr) })
# Knit from the repo root so relative paths resolve.
knitr::opts_knit$set(root.dir = normalizePath(".."))
read_or_null <- function(p) if (file.exists(p)) fread(p) else NULL
```

<!--
For submission: the sections below (Objective through Reproducibility) are the ~1-page
core for the Google Doc. Render to Word (`output_format = "word_document"`), upload the
.docx to Google Drive, and "Open with Google Docs" so tables and figures convert cleanly
(browser copy-paste tends to drop the figures). Part 1 (the conceptual question) is added
directly in the Doc, not here.
-->

## 1. Objective and data

We analyse **`r {x <- read_or_null("data/metadata/pools.tsv"); if (is.null(x)) "N" else nrow(x)}` pooled nasal-swab pools** from SecureBio's Zephyr program
(`https://data.securebio.org/zephyr`). Each pool is untargeted ONT metatranscriptomic
sequencing; the public FASTA per pool contains **long reads already classified as
vertebrate-infecting viruses** after human-read removal. The questions: across >=10 pools,
**what viruses are present and how confident are we**, and **what genome coverage** do we
see for common respiratory viruses.

Because the reads are a pre-filtered, pooled subset, this is best framed as **taxonomic
confirmation and coverage assessment**, not de novo discovery. Abundance (read counts)
reflects sequencing yield, not clinical prevalence, and cannot be attributed to individuals.

## 2. Methods (brief)

- **Read stats:** `seqkit stats`.
- **Taxonomy:** `minimap2 -x map-ont` of each pool against a curated panel of respiratory-virus
  RefSeq genomes; best primary hit per read; per-alignment identity = matches / block length.
- **Coverage:** `minimap2` -> `samtools sort/coverage` (breadth, mean depth) and
  `bedtools genomecov` (depth profiles).
- **Confidence tiers (heuristic):** combine read support, median identity, and genome breadth
  (see Section 3 / README). Members of the same viral family co-called in a pool are flagged
  for possible cross-mapping.
- **Track B clustering:** canonical tetranucleotide (k=4) frequency vectors per read ->
  PCA + UMAP -> k-means / DBSCAN / hierarchical; compared to the minimap2 labels via the
  adjusted Rand index. All steps are R-first; shell tools run in a single conda environment.

## 3. Part 2 - Viruses present, confidence, and coverage

```{r calls}
calls <- read_or_null("results/tables/taxonomy_calls.tsv")
if (!is.null(calls)) {
  top <- head(calls[order(factor(confidence, c("high","medium","low")), -n_reads)], 20)
  kable(top[, .(pool_id, virus, n_reads, median_identity, breadth_pct, mean_depth,
                confidence, ambiguity_flag)],
        caption = "Top per-pool viral calls with confidence tiers (truncated to 20 rows).")
} else {
  cat("_Run the pipeline to populate results/tables/taxonomy_calls.tsv._")
}
```

```{r heatmap, out.width="100%"}
if (file.exists("results/figures/taxonomy_heatmap.png")) {
  knitr::include_graphics("results/figures/taxonomy_heatmap.png")
}
```

**Coverage.** Breadth and depth per virus/segment are summarised below and plotted across the
panel; deep, broadly covered genomes support high-confidence calls, while narrow single-region
hits are reported cautiously.

```{r coverage, out.width="100%"}
cov <- read_or_null("results/tables/coverage_summary.tsv")
if (!is.null(cov)) {
  kable(head(cov[order(-breadth_pct)], 15),
        caption = "Best-covered references (truncated to 15 rows).")
}
if (file.exists("results/figures/coverage_breadth.png")) {
  knitr::include_graphics("results/figures/coverage_breadth.png")
}
if (file.exists("results/figures/coverage_profiles.png")) {
  knitr::include_graphics("results/figures/coverage_profiles.png")
}
```

**Panel recovery.** As a check on how completely the curated panel explains each pool's
pre-filtered viral reads, we report the on- vs off-panel (unmapped) read fraction per pool.

```{r panel-recovery, out.width="100%"}
rec <- read_or_null("results/tables/panel_recovery.tsv")
if (!is.null(rec)) {
  kable(rec[, .(pool_id, total_reads, pct_on_panel, pct_offpanel)],
        caption = "Fraction of each pool's pre-filtered viral reads explained by the panel.")
}
if (file.exists("results/figures/panel_recovery.png")) {
  knitr::include_graphics("results/figures/panel_recovery.png")
}
```

*Interpretation.* **Seasonal human coronaviruses are the clearest signal.** HCoV-229E
dominates pools 02, 04, 06, 08 and 09 (~78k-100k reads each) with near-complete breadth
(75-100%) and very deep coverage (~8,000-12,000x), and HCoV-NL63 dominates pool11 (~100k reads,
88% breadth); HCoV-HKU1 reaches a near-complete genome in pool03 (1,138 reads, 99.7% breadth).
At ONT-typical identity (~0.97-0.99) these are unambiguous, near-complete genomes and the bulk
of the **high**-confidence calls.

**Rhinovirus is ubiquitous but tier-"low" for a reference-divergence reason, not absence.**
Rhinovirus-A/B/C appear in nearly every pool, frequently as the most abundant taxon (e.g. pool10
RV-A 96,854 reads at 99.7% breadth; pool14 RV-A at 100% breadth), yet they fall to **low**
confidence because median identity is only ~0.69-0.78, below the 0.80/0.90 gates. Rhinovirus is
extremely diverse (>160 genotypes) and the single RV-A/B/C RefSeq is divergent from circulating
strains, so reads map at low identity while still covering the whole ~7.1 kb genome deeply. The
high breadth and depth strongly support genuine presence; this is the clearest case where
identity alone should not gate a call, and the tier should be read alongside breadth/depth.

**Sporadic co-detections populate the medium/low tiers.** SARS-CoV-2 is seen in several pools but
mostly at low read counts and narrow breadth (pools 03/04/06/10), with one solid call in pool08
(124 reads, 41% breadth, 0.99 identity). hMPV is **high** in pool15 (612 reads, 70% breadth) and
**medium** elsewhere; influenza A/B appear at low-to-medium support (segmented, so the reported
breadth is the best single segment). Most picornavirus and coronavirus rows carry
`ambiguity_flag = TRUE` because multiple family members co-occur per pool: the dominant member is
clear from read share and breadth, while minor co-members (a few reads, low breadth) are likely
cross-mapping/spillover and are reported cautiously rather than silently resolved.

**Coverage shape supports the calls.** Top hits give near-complete genomes; coronavirus depth
profiles show a 5' peak and a pronounced rise toward the 3' end (consistent with abundant
subgenomic mRNAs in metatranscriptomes), while rhinovirus profiles are deep and broad across the
~7 kb genome with sporadic vertical dropouts at divergent sites. Low-read viruses yield only
patchy single-region coverage, which the tiers correctly down-weight. Panel recovery is near-total
in CoV-dominated pools (>99% on-panel in pools 02/06/09/11) but much lower where divergent
rhinovirus or off-panel viruses prevail (pool13 36%, pool15 34%); these large off-panel fractions
mark the boundary of the curated panel and are the natural targets for the optional BLAST
spot-check.

## 4. Part 3 (Track B) - k-mer embedding and clustering

```{r cluster}
metrics <- read_or_null("results/tables/cluster_metrics.tsv")
if (!is.null(metrics)) {
  kable(metrics, caption = "Cluster-vs-taxonomy concordance (adjusted Rand index).")
}
```

```{r cluster-fig, out.width="49%", fig.show="hold"}
for (f in c("results/figures/kmer_umap_taxonomy.png",
            "results/figures/kmer_umap_clusters.png")) {
  if (file.exists(f)) knitr::include_graphics(f)
}
```

*Interpretation.* **K-mer composition recovers the broad taxonomy but over-splits each
taxon.** On the 3,616 minimap2-labelled reads, agreement with the taxonomy is moderate:
adjusted Rand index 0.42 (hierarchical, ward.D2), 0.40 (k-means, k=8), and 0.05 (DBSCAN).
The UMAP shows the expected high-level structure - coronaviruses and picornaviruses occupy
distinct regions, HCoV-NL63 forms its own clean island, and rhinovirus A/B/C sit together
apart from the coronaviruses. Every k-means cluster is essentially single-species *pure*, so
the modest ARI is driven almost entirely by **fragmentation, not mixing**: HCoV-229E is split
across four clusters (1, 4, 5, 8) and Rhinovirus-A across three (3, 6, 7). This is the expected
failure mode of a per-read tetranucleotide embedding - reads from one genome differ in length
and which sub-region (e.g. 5' vs 3'/subgenomic) they cover, so a single taxon spreads into
several composition clusters. ARI penalises that splitting even though purity is high, which is
why clustering here is confirmatory rather than a replacement for alignment.

**The clusters independently support the Part 2 rhinovirus call.** The `unassigned` reads do
not form their own group; they co-cluster tightly with rhinovirus (clusters 3, 6, and
especially 7: 567 unassigned alongside 290 RV-C / 212 RV-A / 26 RV-B). Composition therefore
places the off-panel/unassigned reads squarely with the picornaviruses, consistent with the
Part 2 reading that these are **divergent rhinovirus mapping below the identity gate**, not a
novel taxon. **DBSCAN effectively fails** (ARI 0.05): the median-4-NN `eps` collapses most
reads into one component plus noise, so density-based clustering does not suit this manifold -
the partition-based methods are the meaningful comparison.

## 5. Limitations

- Results shown here are from a **100,000-read-per-pool subsample** (fast path; pool15 was already
  smaller at 76,508), so absolute depth and breadth are lower than a full-data run. The calls and
  the relative patterns across pools are the intended output; absolute genome completeness is not.
- Reads are **pre-filtered to viruses** and **pooled**: this is confirmation, not discovery,
  and not prevalence.
- **Reference-panel bias:** anything outside the curated panel is `unassigned`. An off-panel
  remote-BLAST spot-check (the unmapped reads of high-off-panel pools 13/14/15 against NCBI `nt`,
  via `scripts/05_blast_confirm.sh ... offpanel`) is **implemented but not completed here**: remote
  BLAST of long ONT reads against `nt` was too slow to finish within the task's time budget.
  Identifying what the large off-panel fractions actually are is the clearest follow-up.
- **ONT error (~5-15%)** lowers identity; tiers use relaxed thresholds, and identity alone is
  not used to call a virus.
- **Close relatives** (seasonal coronaviruses, influenza segments) can cross-map; such cases are
  flagged, not silently resolved.
- **Segmented viruses:** per-virus breadth/depth in the calls table is the best single segment
  (max across segments), not whole-genome coverage; see `coverage_summary.tsv` for per-segment detail.
- Per-read **k-mer clusters are noisy** and may track read length/composition, not only taxonomy.

## 6. Reproducibility

Single conda environment (`environment.yml`); fixed run order in the README/`Makefile` (confidence
calls run after coverage, via the `calls` target); seeds set for UMAP/k-means. The committed results
were generated on a **100k-read/pool subsample** (`MAX_READS=100000 THREADS=12`, seed 11); the
full-data run uses the same commands without `MAX_READS`. Confidence thresholds are tunable defaults
documented in the README, not ground truth.
